
# Acknowledgements {.unnumbered}

> "The more we learn, the easier the human world becomes—but the natural world only grows more wondrous in its complexity"

First, I would like to express my deepest gratitude to the kōura (freshwater crayfish) and the giants on whose shoulders I stood to advance the study of kōura in Aotearoa New Zealand. In particular, I thank Alan A. Devcich, Laura Beverley Francis, Grant E. Barnes, Brendan J. Hicks, C. L. Hopkins, I. G. Jowett, Karin Olsson, Per Nyström, Stephanie M. Parkyn, Nisikawa Usio, Colin R. Townsend, and many others whose foundational work has shaped our understanding of this species. It has been a privilege to build upon their contributions and contribute to the conservation of kōura.

I am profoundly thankful to my supervisors, Deniz Özkundakci, Frank Burdon, Ian Kusabs, and Robin Holmes for their mentorship, quick reviewing processes, and unwavering support throughout this PhD journey. I also extend my appreciation to co-authors, Calum MacNeil and Finnbar Lee, for their collaboration and insights. This research would not have been possible without the support and collaboration of Te Arawa Lakes Trust and Te Komiti Whakahaere. I am especially grateful to Soweeta Fort-D’ath, William Anaru (Te Arawa Lakes Trust), and Tihini Grant (Ngāti Pikiao)

My sincere thanks also extend to the Tāheke Opatia Marae, Te Takinga Marae, and landowners, Piripi Curtis, David McIlraith, Piki Thomas, Haddon Lock, Barnett Vercoe, Richard Vercoe, and Caroline Newton, for granting access to monitoring sites and facilitating fieldwork in Lake Rotoiti.

This research was made possible through funding from the Fish Futures programme, supported by a Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment grant (CAWX2101), with additional funding from the Bay of Plenty Regional Council under the Toihuarewa Waimāori - Bay of Plenty Regional Council Chair in Lake and Freshwater Science programme. I also acknowledge the contributions of Joe Butterworth, Cory O’Neill (Te Arawa Lakes Trust), Alex Simes (Te Arawa Lakes Trust), Martin Sarkezi, Mael Marguet, Ethan Morgan, Logan Kallam, and Matt Prentice, whose assistance with fieldwork, data collection, and logistical support was indispensable. Special thanks to Dion Morgen for his friendship and help in constructing wood splits and blocks.

During my PhD, I became increasingly aware of the limitations of the current scientific publishing system, with paywalls restricting access, and a general lack of transparency in how research is evaluated and cited. This conviction that science should be open, accessible, and collaborative led me to found ScienceEcosystem.org, an open science platform working to lower barriers to knowledge sharing. I remain committed to developing it further in service of that goal.

## Use of Artificial Intelligence
Generative AI tools (Claude, Anthropic) were used during this research to assist with R code development for data analysis and visualisation, and to support editing of written sections through feedback on structure and clarity. All text was written by the author; AI assistance was limited to reorganisation and grammatical suggestions. All analytical decisions, interpretations, and conclusions are the author's own. AI-generated code was reviewed and validated against expected outputs.


::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
\clearpage
\tableofcontents
\clearpage
\mainmatter
:::
